entry.title: Brauche ich Application Management für SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition?
entry.content:
SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition verändert das Betriebsmodell grundlegend. Viele technische Aufgaben, die früher intern oder durch Dienstleister erbracht wurden, liegen im Public-Cloud-Modell bei SAP. Infrastruktur, standardisierte Updates und zentrale Lifecycle-Aktivitäten werden deutlich vereinfacht.

Genau daraus entsteht nach dem Go-live häufig eine berechtigte Frage:

Wenn SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition bereits von SAP betrieben wird - warum sollte ich dann zusätzlich Application Management von SAP benötigen?

Die kurze Antwort lautet: nicht immer, aber häufig. Denn Public Cloud reduziert technische Komplexität, nimmt Unternehmen aber nicht die Verantwortung für ihre Geschäftsprozesse, Benutzer, Integrationen und kontinuierliche Weiterentwicklung.

Dieser Beitrag soll eine praktische Einordnung für Kunden, Partner und Projektteams, die sich mit dem Betrieb nach dem Go-live beschäftigen.

Public Cloud reduziert Aufwand - aber nicht Verantwortung

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition entwickelt sich kontinuierlich weiter. Kunden profitieren von regelmäßigen Innovationen, neuen Funktionen und Verbesserungen. Gleichzeitig müssen diese Änderungen bewertet, getestet, kommuniziert und in den Fachbereichen verankert werden.

Auch nach dem Go-live bleiben zentrale Aufgaben bestehen:

  • Geschäftsprozesse benötigen klare Ownership.
  • Benutzer benötigen Unterstützung im Tagesgeschäft.
  • Integrationen müssen überwacht und analysiert werden.
  • Berechtigungen, Rollen, Formulare, Workflows und Analytics müssen gepflegt werden.
  • Quartalsweise Releases müssen bewertet und getestet werden.
  • Dokumentationen und Enablement-Materialien müssen aktuell bleiben.

SAP Cloud ALM stellt hierfür ein starkes Toolset bereit - etwa für Implementierung, Testing, Deployment, Monitoring und Alerting. Entscheidend ist jedoch, wer die Informationen aus diesen Tools bewertet, priorisiert und in konkrete Maßnahmen übersetzt. Ein Toolset ersetzt kein Betriebsmodell.

Was AMS in diesem Kontext bedeutet

Application Management Services, kurz AMS, sind Post-Go-live-Services zur Stabilisierung, Unterstützung und kontinuierlichen Verbesserung einer SAP-Lösung unter vereinbarten Service Levels.

Im Kontext von SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition geht es weniger um klassische Basis-Administration. Der Schwerpunkt liegt auf Business Application Operations: Prozesse stabil halten, Benutzer unterstützen, Incidents analysieren, Releases vorbereiten, Tests koordinieren und Verbesserungen strukturiert vorantreiben.

Anders gesagt: SAP betreibt die Cloud. Die Frage ist, wer den laufenden Betrieb der Software sicherstellt.

Wann AMS besonders sinnvoll sein kann

AMS wird besonders relevant, wenn SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition geschäftskritische Prozesse unterstützt und operative Lücken vermieden werden müssen.

Typische Auslöser sind:

  • Finanz-, Beschaffungs-, Vertriebs-, Logistik-, Fertigungs- oder Projektprozesse sind geschäftskritisch.
  • Es bestehen mehrere Integrationen zu SAP BTP, Non-SAP-Systemen, Banken, Steuerplattformen, E-Commerce- oder Warehouse-Lösungen.
  • Das interne Team ist klein oder bereits stark ausgelastet.
  • Es fehlt Kapazität für strukturierte Release-Bewertung und Regressionstests.
  • Es werden messbare Service Levels für Incident Handling benötigt.
  • Monitoring, Issue Triage und Fehlerbehebung sind geschäftskritisch.
  • Der Fokus soll nicht nur auf Fehlerbehebung, sondern auch auf kontinuierlicher Verbesserung liegen.

In solchen Situationen kann AMS helfen, Verantwortlichkeiten zu klären, Risiken zu reduzieren und den Wert der Cloud-Lösung langfristig zu sichern.

Fragen zur Einordnung des Betriebsmodells

Für die Zeit nach dem Go-live lohnt sich ein nüchterner Blick auf die eigenen Verantwortlichkeiten und Kapazitäten. Die folgenden Fragen können helfen, das Betriebsmodell realistisch einzuschätzen:

  1. Können Business User nach dem Go-live schnell und zuverlässig unterstützt werden?
  2. Gibt es genügend Kapazität, jedes Release zu bewerten und kritische Prozesse zu testen?
  3. Ist klar geregelt, wer Integrationen, Berechtigungen, Formulare, Workflows und Analytics betreut?
  4. Kann das Team unterscheiden, ob ein Thema aus Produktverhalten, Konfiguration, Prozessdesign, Datenqualität oder Nutzung entsteht?
  5. Gibt es nachvollziehbare Service Levels und Governance für den Anwendungssupport?
  6. Ist definiert, wie Monitoring, Alerting und Incident Triage im Alltag funktionieren?
  7. Können Innovationen übernommen werden, ohne das Tagesgeschäft zu überlasten?

Wenn mehrere dieser Punkte offen sind, ist das kein Zeichen für ein schlechtes Setup. Es zeigt lediglich, dass das Betriebsmodell nach dem Go-live noch weiter konkretisiert werden sollte - unabhängig davon, ob diese Aufgaben intern, durch Partner oder im Rahmen zusätzlicher Application-Management-Fähigkeiten abgedeckt werden.

Fazit

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition nimmt Kunden viel technischen Betriebsaufwand ab. Die Verantwortung für Geschäftsprozesse, Benutzer, Integrationen und kontinuierliche Verbesserung bleibt jedoch bestehen.

AMS von SAP – also SAP Cloud Application Services - können helfen, diese Lücke zu schließen. Sie unterstützen dabei, aus einer bereitgestellten Cloud-Lösung einen stabil betriebenen, geschäftlich verankerten und kontinuierlich verbesserten Anwendungsbetrieb zu machen.

Die entscheidende Frage lautet daher nicht:

„Ist SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition nicht schon gemanagt?“

Sondern:

„Wer stellt sicher, dass unsere Geschäftsanwendung jeden Tag stabil läuft, Releases beherrscht werden und Innovation tatsächlich im Business ankommt?“

Wenn diese Verantwortung intern klar besetzt und ausreichend skalierbar ist, kann das genügen. Wenn nicht, sind SAP Cloud Application Services eine sinnvolle Ergänzung des Betriebsmodells.

Was bedeutet das für die Praxis?

Wenn Ihr SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition nutzt oder Euch auf den Go-live vorbereitet, lohnt sich ein ehrlicher Blick auf Euer zukünftiges Betriebsmodell.

Klärt frühzeitig, welche Aufgaben bei SAP liegen, was intern übernommen wird, welche Rolle Partner spielen und wo zusätzliche Application-Management-Fähigkeiten Stabilität, Geschwindigkeit und kontinuierliche Verbesserung unterstützen können.

Hinweise und weiterführende Links

Weitere Informationen und Materialien findet Ihr hier:

  • SAP Cloud Application Services auf sap.com: https://www.sap.com/germany/services-support/cloud-application-services.html
  • SAP Cloud Application Services im Partner Portal: https://partneredge.sap.com/en/partnership/services/cas.html
  • Webinar Aufzeichnung: Autonomous Enterprise beginnt im Betrieb - der konkrete Mehrwert von SAP Cloud Application Services

entry.title: Why Doesn't My Purchase Requisition Commitment Go to Zero? Demystifying Budget Commitment Behavior
SAP S/4HANA Cloud and SAP SCM Enablement: Live Product Expert Sessions in 2026
entry.content:
As organizations accelerate their journey toward the Autonomous Enterprise, SAP is redefining how business processes are executed through AI, automation, and data-driven decision-making. With SAP’s recent Autonomous Enterprise vision now rolled out, enabling users to fully leverage SAP S/4HANA Cloud and SAP Supply Chain Management (SCM) solutions has become more important than ever.

We at Cloud ERP & SCM Product Success Enablement and Adoption strive to enable our valued customers and partners to take advantage of our suite solutions. Therefore, we offer various enablement assets, from product experts to product experts, explaining the features and functions as well as the benefits of innovations across the suite.

In this blog, we showcase the diverse live session formats delivered for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, and SAP Supply Chain Management. From expert-led webinars to deep-dive learning experiences, these programs are designed to help customers and partners stay current with innovations, build practical expertise, and accelerate adoption of SAP S/4HANA Cloud and SAP SCM solutions. Explore how each format supports different learning needs and empowers participants to get the most value from their SAP investments.

 

In the following, I'd like to shed some light on our various live product expert sessions. As of today, the team offers five different types of live sessions:

  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition: Early Release Series | Equivalent: What’s New for SAP SCM
  • In-Depth with SAP Cloud ERP | Equivalent: Meet the Expert for SAP SCM
  • Product Expert Training for the Autonomous Enterprise

 

This table helps you to get an overview of the available sessions as well as the content that is covered during the different formats. If you're interested in more detail and would like to follow regular updates, check out the individual landing pages linked below.

Live Product Expert Session

Content Covered

Frequency

Target Audience

Access Requirements

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition: Early Release Series

Talk about and demo key innovations for upcoming major SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition releases

Bi-yearly

Customers, partners, SAP Internals

Active SAP Learning Hub License needed*

In-Depth with SAP Cloud ERP

Deep-dive knowledge transfer sessions independently from releases for both public and private cloud

Monthly based on demand

Customers, partners, SAP Internals

Active SAP Learning Hub License needed*

What’s New for SAP SCM

Talk about and demo key innovations for major SAP SCM releases

Bi-yearly

Customers, partners, SAP Internals

ON24 webinars, open & free for anyone

Meet the Expert for SAP SCM

Deep-dive knowledge transfer sessions independently from releases for SAP SCM

Monthly based on demand

Customers, partners, SAP Internals

ON24 webinars, open & free for anyone

Product Expert Training H1

Snackable standalone expert videos covering broad and deep-dive product knowledge across the suite

Always-on in H1 starting mid-March – end of June

Open to anyone

Via learning.sap.com

Product Expert Training H2

~ 3 full weeks of training covering broad and deep-dive product knowledge across the suite

Hybrid event in H2 (starting 28th Sept. 2026)

Partners and SAP internals

Active SAP Learning Hub License needed**

*Get access to Support Edition for Customers & Partners // Internal Edition for SAP Employees.
**Access Partner Edge here or send a mail to partner@sap.com if you face any issues.

 

Live Product Expert Sessions Throughout 2026

Fig. 1: Global enablement plan for 2026; based on SAP’s release plan. As the release plan may be changed by SAP without notice, the global enablement plans can change as well.

 

With the above focus of the sessions in mind, let's take a look at the global live session plan for 2026. For the two major releases SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602 and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2608, we plan on showing and demoing highlights of the releases during the Early Release Series which is happening one week before shipment.

Similar to the offering for the Public Cloud, we host a webinar series to cover the latest features in the area of SAP SCM via the What’s New format.

In an almost monthly cadence, based on demand, we're hosting In-Depth with SAP Cloud ERP which is a format that is running independently from releases. In these sessions, we cover specific topics in-depth to get a more holistic insight into certain aspects of the product. As this series is not bound to releases, it depends on topic suggestions from our stakeholders. If you'd like to nominate topics, simply do so via this blog post.

Similar to this In-Depth series, we host an event called Meet the Expert which is targeted for deep dive insights into SAP SCM solutions.

In H1 2026, we offer the Product Expert Training in an always-on format with standalone expert videos and bite-sized learning content covering the Autonomous Suite. In the second half of the year (H2) 2026, the Product Expert Training for the Autonomous Suite will take place in a hybrid mode. The event will combine an onsite week in St. Leon-Rot with multiple weeks of virtual sessions, enabling partners to engage directly with SAP product experts and executives in person, while also allowing broader participation through live online access.

 

 

How to sign up for the SAP Enterprise Support Edition as a customer/partner

In order to watch the recordings, you will need to have an active SAP Learning Hub license with at least the SAP Enterprise Support Edition. Find out how to register for the SAP Enterprise Support edition in case you don't already have access to any of the editions yet:

  • Navigate to the sign up page
  • Click the Sign up button. Authenticate yourself with your s-user ID and password. Upon first access, the system will check your eligibility, create a new SAP Learning Hub user, and populate your learning catalog respectively. Within two hours, you will then receive a registration confirmation via e-mail.
  • After you have received the confirmation e-mail, the registration process is complete.

Partners can access the recordings through an SAP Partner Edge Edition.

 

How to sign up for the Internal Edition as an SAP employee

In order to watch the recordings, you will need to have an SAP Learning Hub license. In case you have no edition assigned to your profile, please register here for the Internal Edition which is free of charge.

Make sure to be logged onto the SAP Learning Hub before opening the URL. In case the link doesn't open, copy and paste it into your browser. If you face any other issues with your login, please contact the help center.

 

Follow the PSCC_Enablement tag to stay up to date with the latest blog posts.

 

Find All of Our Assets

Is there more enablement we’re offering? Of course! We offer a podcast, different video formats, demo playlists, customer councils and much more. Check out our interactive wheel:

entry.title: Setting Up SAP Joule on S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition: A Technical Guide
entry.content:
Introduction

SAP Joule is SAP’s generative AI copilot designed to enhance user productivity across SAP applications. In SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition, Joule empowers users to discover applications, access contextual business insights, and navigate business processes conversationally directly within the SAP Fiori experience.

This document provides a comprehensive, end-to-end technical guide for setting up SAP Joule with SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition. It is written from an administrator and platform engineering perspective, explaining not only how to configure each component, but also why it is required and how the overall integration functions.

Architecture Overview

 

At a high level, Joule runs on SAP BTP and is embedded into the SAP user experience using SAP Build Work Zone. Authentication and authorization are handled centrally through SAP Cloud Identity Services.

Prerequisites and Readiness Checklist

Before proceeding with the setup, ensure the following prerequisites are in place:

Authorizations

  • SAP BTP Global Account Administrator
  • SAP BTP Subaccount Administrator
  • SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS & IPS) Administrator
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Administrator

Entitlements

  • SAP Joule (Digital Assistant Service – das-application)
  • SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition

Landscape Requirements

  • Supported for both 2-System Landscape (2SL) and 3-System Landscape (3SL)
  • SAP Cloud Connector is not required for Public Cloud systems

Technical Preconditions

  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud tenant registered in BTP System Landscape
  • SAP Cloud Identity Services tenant available and accessible
  • Browser allows iFrames and third-party cookies

Preparation

SAP BTP Subaccount Strategy

A dedicated SAP BTP subaccount is typically used to host Joule and SAP Build Work Zone. From a design perspective, regional alignment with SAP Cloud Identity Services is recommended to minimize authentication latency and reduce cross-region dependencies.

The subaccount acts as the orchestration layer where:

  • Joule services are provisioned
  • Work Zone acts as the user entry point
  • Trust and identity integration converge

Identity Trust and Domain Alignment
Single Sign-On between SAP S/4HANA Cloud, SAP BTP, and SAP Build Work Zone is achieved through trust establishment with SAP Cloud Identity Services.

A critical design consideration here is domain alignment:

  • The IAS domain selected must align with the domain model of the SAP S/4HANA Cloud tenant
  • Mismatched domains are a frequent root cause of repeated authentication prompts and intermittent Joule access issues

Trusted domain configuration on both BTP and IAS is essential for enabling secure cross-origin communication and embedded user experiences.

Identity Attribute Readiness
Joule and SAP Build Work Zone rely on a consistent identity model provided by IAS. Certain core user attributes must be available and consumable by downstream applications.

From an operational perspective:

  • Attribute completeness matters more than attribute volume
  • Missing verification-related attributes can result in silent authentication failures that are difficult to trace at runtime

Ensuring identity consistency early on significantly reduces troubleshooting effort later.

Joule Enablement Model
Guided Onboarding via Booster

SAP provides a Joule Booster that automates the majority of the technical enablement steps. This guided onboarding mechanism establishes the baseline configuration required for Joule to operate within the BTP subaccount.

Conceptually, the booster:

  • Validates entitlements and platform readiness
  • Establishes system formations
  • Creates the necessary connectivity artifacts
  • Activates SAP-delivered communication scenarios

 

Architecturally, this step should be treated as a managed onboarding process rather than a manual configuration exercise.

SAP Build Work Zone Integration

SAP Build Work Zone serves as the experience layer through which Joule is surfaced to end users. Key design elements include:

  • Subscription to the Work Zone service
  • Secure consumption of S/4HANA Cloud content
  • Centralized role-based navigation and access control.

Work Zone consumes S/4HANA Cloud content using SAP-delivered exposure mechanisms, ensuring that only authorized applications, catalogs, and groups are visible.

Content Exposure Model
Content exposure from SAP S/4HANA Cloud into SAP Build Work Zone is governed by standard communication scenarios delivered by SAP. These scenarios enable controlled sharing of Fiori content without custom integration.

From a best-practice perspective:

  • Content exposure should be validated, not modified
  • Role and catalog assignments remain the authoritative control mechanism

This approach preserves clean-core principles while enabling rich user experiences.

Identity Provisioning Architecture
Identity Provisioning Services (IPS) are used to synchronize users and role assignments into SAP Build Work Zone.

Rather than being treated as a one-time activity, identity provisioning should be viewed as:

  • A continuous synchronization mechanism
  • A dependency for consistent authorization behavior
  • A key operational component for Joule visibility

Operational validation of IPS jobs and logs is a critical checkpoint during the initial enablement.

Validation and Operational Readiness
Once the platform setup is complete, validation should focus on the end-to-end user experience rather than on individual configuration items.

Typical validation checkpoints include:

  • Successful single sign-on from SAP S/4HANA Cloud
  • Visibility of SAP Build Work Zone content
  • Availability of the Joule interaction entry point
  • Execution of basic conversational queries

A successful validation confirms that Joule is correctly embedded and operational.

 

Common Operational Observations
Based on real-world implementations, the following patterns are frequently observed:

  • Repeated login prompts usually indicate trust or domain misalignment
  • Missing Joule entry points are commonly linked to role assignment or identity synchronization gaps
  • Empty or incomplete content typically points to content exposure or IPS issues

Systematically addressing these areas resolves the majority of post-enablement issues.

entry.title: Get Ahead of What's Next: Join the Early Release Series
entry.content:
Be First to Know What’s Next

Curious about the innovations shaping the future of supply chain? The Early Release Series gives you a front row seat. It's a biannual event where SAP product experts walk you through what's coming next in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, before everyone else.

What You'll Get Out of It

Across several days, you can join expert-led demos, gain insider insights, and take part in real-time Q&A. That means early access to new features and direct answers from the people building them. It's a practical way to see live demos, ask your questions, and plan ahead with confidence.

Sessions for the SCM Community

Here are four sessions we think you'll find valuable. Each one is a detailed, expert-led deep dive.

Artificial Intelligence — July 13, 2026, 12:30pm – 2:00pm CET

Take a broader look at where AI is heading. This session covers the Autonomous Enterprise, Joule for Business, workflow automation, and Autonomous SCM, including production, planning, and inventory agents that connect directly to your supply chain work.

Supply Chain Management — July 16, 2:00pm – 2:45pm CET

Discover the latest innovations in Supply Chain Management and see how they can support your day-to-day operations.

Manufacturing — July 14, 4:00pm – 4:45pm CET

Explore the newest manufacturing innovations, from engineering and planning to shop-floor execution. You'll see how AI-driven manufacturing agents help streamline decisions, automate routine tasks, and strengthen operational resilience. Expect practical demos, insider tips, and a clear view of what's next, so you can accelerate value from day one.

Quality Management — July 16, 4:30pm – 5:15pm CET

Get up to speed on the latest innovations in Quality Management and what they mean for your processes.

 

How to Sign Up

An active SAP Learning Hub license is required to register for the live sessions and to watch the recordings afterward.

If you're a customer:

You'll need access to at least the SAP Enterprise Support edition. Here's how to sign up:

  1. Go to the sign-up page and select Sign up.
  2. Authenticate with your S-user ID and password.
  3. The system checks your eligibility, creates your SAP Learning Hub user, and populates your learning catalogue. This step can take up to 2 hours.
  4. You'll get a confirmation e-mail once registration is complete.

If you're a partner:

With a valid SAP Universal ID and access to the Empowering SAP Partners site, you can join the live sessions and recordings directly.

Don't Miss Out

These sessions won't wait, and neither should you. Secure your spot to gain early insights, watch live demos, and stay ahead with the newest SAP updates.

Register today and be first to know what's next: learning.sap.com

entry.title: SAP Green Ledger: Q2-26 Release Updates and Highlights
entry.content:
Welcome to the Q2 2026 edition of our SAP Green Ledger Quarterly Release Highlights! This blog summarizes the most important SAP Green Ledger innovations and enhancements delivered during Q2 2026. Highlights include new capabilities for carbon traceability, carbon collection processing, Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM), carbon allocations, analytics, and usability improvements across SAP Green Ledger.

Read on to learn more about the key innovations introduced throughout Q2 2026:

  • Carbon Traceability with Related SAP S/4HANA Documents
  • Mass Reversal of Carbon Collections
  • Simplified CBAM Processing
  • Flexible Carbon Allocations
  • Analytical Dashboard Update
  • Further Updates and Enhancements

 

Carbon Traceability with Related SAP S/4HANA Documents

The Manage Carbon Collections app now includes a new "Related Documents" section that allows you to maintain and view references to the SAP S/4HANA documents from which emissions originate. Supported reference types include:

  • Purchasing Documents
  • Accounting Documents
  • Other Documents

If you import material movements into SAP Green Ledger from a connected SAP S/4HANA system, related document references are transferred automatically. When importing carbon data via CSV files, you can also include related document information in the file. For manually created carbon collections, you can maintain related documents directly in the Manage Carbon Collections app. These references are then carried through from the carbon collection into the corresponding journal entries in the Manage Journal Entries app. This allows you to trace carbon postings throughout the accounting process, from the originating business documents to the resulting carbon journal entries, also improving auditability of carbon data.

Related Documents in a Carbon CollectionTo further simplify navigation and reporting, you can now also filter in the Manage Carbon Collections, Manage Journal Entries and Display Line Items apps by Related Document Type and Related Document ID. This helps you quickly locate carbon records for specific business transactions. For more details check SAP Help: Create a Carbon Collection and Use a CSV Template.

 

Mass Reversal of Carbon Collections

The Manage Carbon Collections app now supports reversing multiple posted carbon collections in a single step. Instead of reversing each carbon collection individually, you can now select and reverse multiple posted carbon collections at once. This significantly reduces manual effort, particularly after correcting mass uploads. Up to 5,000 carbon collections can be reversed in a single operation. For more information, see SAP Help: Post a Carbon Collection to the Journal.

 

Simplified CBAM Processing

Several enhancements improve the processing and monitoring of Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM) carbon collections while also increasing transparency for CBAM accounts configuration.

Efficient Processing of CBAM Carbon Collections

The Manage Carbon Collections app now also supports reversing CBAM carbon collections. In addition, you can now post and reverse multiple CBAM carbon collections in a single step. As a result, you can process large volumes of carbon collections that require carbon certificates more efficiently.

When reversing CBAM carbon collections, the resulting journal entries are created with the journal entry source type Carbon Collection Reversal, providing a clear audit trail for correction postings. When posting or reversing multiple CBAM carbon collections, SAP Green Ledger automatically calculates a single fair value per company code. This reduces manual effort and ensures consistent valuation across company codes.

To support the monitoring of these postings, the Manage Carbon Liabilities app now supports filtering carbon liabilities by Posted and Reversed status. With that, you can quickly monitor the processing status of carbon liabilities and identify relevant entries. Check out SAP Help for more details: Post a Carbon Collection to the Journal, Fair Value Calculation, and View Carbon Liabilities.

Filter for Posted and Reversed Carbon Liabilities in the Manage Carbon Liabilities App

Improved Visibility into EU CBAM Accounts by Company Code

The Configure Carbon Certificate Data app now displays the EU CBAM accounts maintained in the Determine Mandatory Accounts app for each company code. This allows you to view the configured account assignments directly while working with carbon certificate data, without switching between applications. See also SAP Help: Configure Carbon Certificate Data and Determine Mandatory Accounts.

 EU CBAM Accounts per Company Code in Configure Carbon Certificate Data App

 

Flexible Carbon Allocations

Several enhancements make carbon allocations more flexible and align them more closely with financial accounting. Our new capabilities can help you reuse existing allocation logic and support project-based allocation scenarios.

Copy Financial Flow for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition

The ability to create allocation models by copying financial flows in the Manage Allocations app is now also available for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition. Previously available only for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, this enhancement enables you to create carbon allocation models based on the allocation patterns already used in your connected SAP S/4HANA system.

Allocation Model Copied from Financial Flow

Using the latest Cost Center Accounting data from the connected SAP S/4HANA system, SAP Green Ledger automatically copies the receiving objects and allocation percentages and transfers them into the allocation model. This eliminates the need to manually maintain allocation rules and helps keep carbon allocations aligned with financial allocations. For more details, check SAP Help: Create Allocation Models by Copying from Financial Flow.

Project-Level Carbon Allocations with WBS Elements

The Manage Allocations app now supports Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) elements as both sender and receiver dimensions when creating periodic allocation models. For both the sender and receiver, you can now choose a WBS element or a cost center as the allocation dimension, enabling allocation scenarios such as WBS-to-WBS, WBS-to-Cost Center, Cost Center-to-WBS, and Cost Center-to-Cost Center. To ensure consistency with the existing allocation behavior for cost centers, the corresponding Profit Center and Segment are now automatically derived as well when a WBS element is used as the receiver dimension.

WBS Elements Used as Sender and Receiver Dimensions in a Periodic Allocation Model

 To further improve transparency, Partner WBS Elements are now included in journal entry line items and displayed in both the Manage Journal Entries and Display Line Items apps. You can also filter, group, and sort by Partner WBS Element in the Display Line Items app, giving you better visibility into carbon allocation flows across project structures. For more details, see Create Period Allocation Models.

 

Analytical Dashboard Update

The SAP Green Ledger Analytical Dashboard has also been updated for Q2 2026 with the latest analytical capabilities for combined financial and carbon reporting. For a detailed overview of these enhancements, see the dedicated SAP Community blog post and the SAP Help Portal topic Analyze Carbon Data Alongside Financial Data.

 

Further Updates and Enhancements

Several smaller enhancements further improve the usability of SAP Green Ledger.

Improved Journal Entry Usability

The Manage Journal Entries app now allows you to maximize the Line Items table. You can review journal entries with many line items more comfortably and reduce the need for scrolling. Additionally, each line item is now assigned a stable unique identifier that remains unchanged when the table is sorted or filtered. The stable identifier makes it easier to reference individual line items and improves navigation during journal analysis.

Updated Posting Terminology

The posting action in the Manage Carbon Collections app has been renamed from Post Carbon Collection to simply Post. The related posting statuses are:

  • Posting
  • Posted
  • Posting Failed
New Origin Business System Filter

A new Origin Business System filter is now available in the Manage Journal Entries, Display Line Items, and Display Account Balances apps. Selecting an origin business system automatically limits the available master data to values from that system. Likewise, when a master data object that is linked to a specific business system, such as a company code, is selected, the corresponding business system is identified automatically and applied to the remaining filters.

 

Additional Information

If you want to dive deeper into SAP Green Ledger, the SAP Help Portal is a good place to start. It provides detailed information on concepts, configuration, and application usage. You can also check the What’s New section, which is updated with every release and gives a complete overview of the latest additions. To see what's coming next, the SAP Road Map Explorer offers insight into planned features and upcoming innovations. It is updated regularly and outlines how the solution will continue to evolve.

entry.title: Using Investment Projects for Larger Maintenance Activities - Including Project Stock
entry.content:
Contents

  1. Background - Why Standard Maintenance Orders Are Sometimes Not Enough
  2. The Standard Investment Project Process (Recap)
  3. Investment Projects for Larger Maintenance Activities
  4. Adding Project-Specific Stock to the Scenario
  5. Variant: Investment Project Without an Investment Profile
  6. Key Takeaways
  7. Further Reading
  8. System Example Showing the Process Flow


1. Background - Why Standard Maintenance Orders Are Sometimes Not Enough

When a company plans a significant maintenance activity, an overhaul of a complex machine, a major refurbishment of production equipment, or a large infrastructure repair, the cost and material management requirements quickly exceed what a simple overhead project can provide comfortably.

Key challenges in these scenarios include:

  • Tracking all incurred costs (internal labor, external services, materials) in one structured object across a potentially multi-month timeline.
  • Ring-fencing materials ordered specifically for this activity so they cannot accidentally be consumed by other production or maintenance tasks.
  • At the end of the activity deciding which portion of the costs should be capitalised to the fixed asset and which should be expensed. (As a big overhaul will increase the value of the asset, capitalising at least part of the maintenance costs makes totally sense.)

Investment projects in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud address all three challenges in a single, coherent setup.

 

2. The Standard Investment Project Process (Recap)

Before focusing on the maintenance use case, it is worth recapping the standard flow because the maintenance scenario builds directly on it.

In a standard investment project, costs for the creation of a new own-used asset are collected on a project and settled to an Asset under Construction (AuC). This settlement to the AuC can be done event-based or period-based. This moves costs off P&L into the balance sheet. Once the asset is ready for use, the AuC is settled to the final fixed asset. Any costs that cannot be capitalised are redirected to a cost center or an overhead project.

  1. Create Investment Project & WBS Structure
    Define the project with an investment profile. The investment profile drives the automatic creation of an Asset under Construction (AuC).
  2. Collect Costs on the Project
    Post actual costs: purchase orders, goods receipts, time confirmations, service entry sheets. All costs land on WBS elements.
  3. Settlement to the AuC
    At period end (or event-based, triggered by every posting to the project), costs are settled from the investment project to the AuC. This removes costs from P&L and places them on the balance sheet.
  4. Final Settlement to the Fixed Asset
    When the asset is complete, the AuC is settled to the final fixed asset. Non-capitalisable costs are distributed to a cost center or overhead project according to the settlement rule.
  5. Asset Activation & Deprecation
    The asset is activated in Asset Accounting and begins regular depreciation according to the assigned depreciation key.

SAP Community Blog Reference
For a detailed walkthrough of the standard settlement process, see the SAP Community blog post: 
Settlement of Investment Projects in SAP Cloud ERP at community.sap.com.

 

3. Investment Projects for Larger Maintenance Activities

Investment projects are not limited to the creation of entirely new assets. They are equally well-suited for larger maintenance activities. For example, a complete overhaul of a turbine, a production line rebuild, or a major refurbishment of a building; where a (substantial) portion of the incurred costs is expected to be capitalised to the existing fixed asset.

Typical Scenario

A company plans an extensive overhaul of a complex machine. The activity is expected to take several weeks, involve multiple vendors and internal teams, and require a large volume of specific spare parts. The total cost is expected to be significant, and management expects that a meaningful portion will extend the useful life or increase the value of the machine - qualifying for capitalisation.

Why an Investment Project Fits

  • Structured cost collection: WBS elements allow costs to be organised by work package/ project phase (e.g., disassembly, repair, reassembly, testing).
  • Flexible settlement: At the end of the overhaul, the settlement rule determines which percentage is posted to the fixed asset (capitalisation) and which goes to a cost center or another cost object (period expense). Both receivers are optional.
  • Audit trail: with event-based settlement all postings are traceable back to their origin - purchase orders, time entries, material consumptions - via the project.

INFO: When to Capitalise Maintenance Costs
Under IFRS and US GAAP, costs that extend the useful life of an asset, increase its capacity, or improve its quality beyond the original standard are capitalisable. Routine repairs that merely maintain the current condition are expensed. The settlement rule in SAP lets you split both in a single settlement run.

Setup Considerations

When the goal is to settle a portion of costs to an existing fixed asset (not create a new one), you have two options:

  • With investment profile — an AuC is created automatically. Costs flow from the project to the AuC during the activity, and finally from the AuC to the existing fixed asset. This approach is preferred when the activity runs across multiple periods and interim balance sheet capitalisation is required.
  • Without investment profile — no AuC is created. Costs accumulate on the project and are settled directly to the existing fixed asset at the end. This simpler variant is covered in Section 5.

 

4. Adding Project-Specific Stock to the Scenario

For large maintenance activities, materials are often ordered weeks in advance and stored in the warehouse. Without further measures, these materials sit in unrestricted stock and can be issued to any other order or project creating the risk that critical spare parts are consumed elsewhere, delaying the overhaul.

Project stock solves this by ring-fencing materials for a specific WBS element. Any goods receipt for a purchase order referencing that WBS element lands in project stock, not in unrestricted stock. Goods issues from that stock are only possible against the same project.

Availability in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud
In SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, project stock is available for investment projects and projects with revenue. This is a deliberate design: overhead cost projects do not support project stock in the public cloud edition.

End-to-End Material Flow with Project Stock

  1. Create Purchase Order with Account Assignment to WBS Element
    The buyer creates a purchase order for the required spare parts. The account assignment references the relevant WBS element of the investment project. The account assignment category is set to Q – Project – Make-to-Order.
  2. Goods Receipt into Project Stock
    When the materials arrive, the goods receipt posts them into project stock (a separate stock segment linked exclusively to the WBS element, special stock indicator Q). The materials are visible in inventory management but are reserved for this project only.
  3. Goods Issue from Project Stock to the Project
    When the materials are needed during the overhaul, they are issued from project stock to the WBS element. The cost hits the investment project directly at actual cost.
  4. Settlement of Material Costs Together with Other Project Costs
    Material costs posted to the WBS element are settled along with all other project costs (labor, services) to the AuC, or directly to the fixed asset, or to other receivers like e.g. cost centers, or to a combination of all of these objects. This can be done using the same settlement rule.

5. Variant: Investment Project Without an Investment Profile

Not every maintenance capitalisation scenario requires the interim step of an Asset under Construction. If the maintenance activity is relatively self-contained and management is comfortable settling directly to the existing fixed asset at completion, an investment project without an investment profile is a simpler and equally valid option.

How It Works

Without an investment profile assigned to the WBS element, no AuC is created automatically. Costs are collected on the project throughout the activity. At the end, the settlement rule points directly to the existing fixed asset (for the capitalisable portion) and to a cost center (for the expensed portion). The settlement is executed once at project close.

INFO: No AuC — But Still an Investment Project
The project must still be created as an investment project (not an overhead cost project) to be eligible for project stock. The absence of an investment profile means no AuC is created, but the project type remains "investment," which is the prerequisite for using project-specific stock in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud.

When to Use This Variant

  • The maintenance activity is completed within a single fiscal period or a short time frame where interim balance sheet capitalisation is not required.
  • You do not need to report work in progress on the balance sheet during the activity.
  • You want to minimise the number of settlement postings and avoid managing an AuC asset master.

Scenario Comparison at a Glance

Aspect

With Investment Profile (AuC)

Without Investment

Asset under Construction created?

Yes — automatically from investment profile

No — no AuC master record

Interim balance sheet capitalisation?

Yes — costs move to balance sheet at each settlement or even event-based

No — costs remain on the cost object until project close

Settlement target

AuC → final fixed asset at project completion

Directly to the existing fixed asset or other receivers at project close

Project stock eligible?

Yes

Yes — investment project type is still required

Partial capitalisation possible?

Yes — via settlement rule percentages

Yes — via settlement rule percentages

Best suited for

Multi-period projects, large capex activities, new asset creation

Shorter maintenance activities, direct asset improvement, simpler setup

 

6. Key Takeaways

  • Investment projects in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud are not restricted to new asset creation, they are an effective tool for managing costs of larger maintenance and refurbishment activities.
  • Project stock is available for investment projects (and revenue projects) in the public cloud edition. This makes the investment project the right vehicle when material ring-fencing is required for a major maintenance task.
  • The settlement rule gives you the flexibility to capitalise a portion of maintenance costs to the existing fixed asset while expensing the remainder to a cost center. It is also possible to expense 100% of the maintenance costs. This can be done in a single settlement run.
  • If interim AuC-based balance sheet capitalisation is not required, an investment project without an investment profile simplifies the setup while retaining all cost-collection and project-stock capabilities.

7. Further Reading

  • SAP Community Blog: Settlement of Investment Projects in SAP Cloud ERP
  • SAP Help Portal: Scope Item 35F — Project Control: Capital Projects (Best Practices)

 

8. System Example Showing the Process Flow

1. Create Capital Investment Project
when creating the project, you need to use the Project Profile: Investment Project,
otherwise the functionality described in this blog will not be available

The assignment of an investment profile is optional.

  • With an investment profile assigned the system will automatically, with release of the project, create an asset under construction (AuC), you will then periodically or event-based settle the costs from the project to the AuC (move costs out of P&L and capitalise them in the balance sheet).
  • If no investment profile is assigned the system will not create an asset under construction. All the costs posted to the investment project can later be settled to a fixed asset, a cost center, another project,…

2. Capture Planned Costs and/ or Project Budget
If needed, you can upload planned costs or budgets to the investment project.
You can use the app Import Financial Plan Data (F1711) to upload planned costs or project budget.
Planned costs and budgets are differentiated via the plan category. Make sure you choose the category BUDGET01 for the budget upload.

3. Create Demands
If needed, demands can be planned for the investment project. Details are described in scope item 4RF- Project Control – Resourcing and Procurement

4. Purchase Materials into Project Stock

  • create a demand (as described in 3.), or
  • directly create a purchase order (app Manage Purchase Orders) with
    - account assignment category Q – Project – Make-to-Order
    - enter the WBS that should own the stock (item details), you do not need to enter a GL account
    The special stock indicator Q - Project Stock is derived automatically from the WBS account assignment.

    If the purchase order needs an approval, check the tab “Approval Details” to find the assigned approver.
    Approvals can be found via Self Services > My Inbox > Approve Purchase Order

5. Goods Receipt for Project Stock

  • Use the app Post Goods Receipt for Purchasing Document (F0843) to post a goods receipt into project stock.
  • Check your project reporting (Project Financial Booklet Booklet – Enterprise Projects) to see the balance sheet posting (material inventory) is assigned/ attributed to the investment project
    The project report shows the inventory account (BS) is account assigned to the project and therefore visible in project reporting. This is only done for project stock.
  • Check your material inventory to see the posted material quantity update in project stock (special stock indicator Q).
    This means, these materials are not freely available but can only be consumed by the assigned WBS element.
    app Stock – Multiple Materials (F1595)

6. Consume Material from Project Stock
Use the app Post Goods Movement (MIGO) to consume the material from project inventory to the investment project.
- Movement Type 221
- Special Stock Indicator Q
enter your WBS element as this project stock can only be consumed for the assigned WBS
the project report shows the consumption:

7. Post Costs to the Investment Project
e.g. activity allocation posting (app Manage Activity Allocation)


8. Maintain Settlement Rules
the settlement rule can be maintained in the project master (app Project Control – Enterprise Projects)

In this example 50% of the costs are capitalised and therefore settled to a fixed asset, the other 50% are settled to a maintenance cost center.
It would also be possible to settle 100% to cost center or other cost objects. There is no need to settle to a fixed asset.

9. Project Settlement
app Run Settlement – Actual

entry.title: Extending SAP Joule with SharePoint Grounding and MCP
entry.content:
What You Will Learn

In this blog post you will learn how to:

  • Ground SAP Joule in SharePoint documents using SAP AI Core Document Grounding so that Joule can answer questions directly from your document library
  • Build a chunking and embedding pipeline in SAP AI Launchpad that keeps the vector index fresh as documents change
  • Create custom Joule skills and agents in SAP Build that chain document search, structured extraction, and S/4HANA API calls into a fully automated workflow — no copy-paste, no form-filling
  • Connect a Model Context Protocol (MCP) server as a Joule toolkit so that warehouse operations can evolve independently of the agent definition

By the end of this two-part series, a warehouse operator can type "Process delivery note 54321" in Joule and have an inbound delivery created in S/4HANA automatically.

Prerequisites

Before you start, make sure you have:

  • An SAP BTP subaccount with entitlements for SAP AI Core (Extended plan) and SAP Build Process Automation
  • An SAP AI Launchpad instance connected to your AI Core service
  • An SAP S/4HANA Cloud tenant with the Inbound Delivery A2X API enabled, or access to SAP API Business Hub sandbox
  • A Microsoft 365 tenant with a SharePoint site containing your delivery note documents (PDF, DOCX, XLSX, or PPTX)
  • Global Administrator or Privileged Role Administrator access in Microsoft Entra ID — or a contact who has it — to grant admin consent for API permissions
  • Basic familiarity with SAP Build (Joule Studio) and BTP destinations

Introduction

SAP Joule is SAP's generative AI copilot embedded across the SAP ecosystem. It understands SAP transactional data well out of the box — but enterprise knowledge often lives outside SAP. Delivery notes, vendor contracts, material specs, and SOPs typically sit in SharePoint, Teams, or network drives. Joule cannot act on what it cannot read.

This walkthrough shows how we connected those two worlds. The result is a Joule Capability Package called Custom Agent Ideation that imports three reusable building-block packages and adds two orchestration agents and four Joule skills on top:

  • searchDeliveryNotes — instant vector search over SharePoint PDFs directly from Joule
  • deliveryNotetoInboundDelivery — end-to-end automation: search → extract → map → create inbound delivery in S/4HANA
  • createInboundDelivery — direct inbound delivery creation when you already have the data
  • warehouseTaskCreator — MCP-powered agent that creates warehouse tasks from an inbound delivery number

Architecture Overview

Joule Extensibility — Reference Architecture

The SAP reference architecture for Integrating and Extending Joule defines the canonical pattern that underpins everything in this walkthrough. At its core, Joule sits in the middle — connected to SAP BTP for extensibility, SAP Cloud Identity Services for authentication, and the broader SAP application estate (S/4HANA Cloud, SuccessFactors, Ariba, Concur) through a unified integration layer.

extend joule with joule studio

Source: SAP Architecture Center — Integrating and Extending Joule (Ref-Arch 464deb)

Joule's extensibility operates across four capability patterns:

Pattern Description

TransactionalNatural language triggers direct backend processes — purchase orders, approvals, document creation
NavigationalGuides users to the right application or screen without manual navigation
InformationalDelivers knowledge from SAP Help, customer documents, or indexed content via RAG
AnalyticalSurfaces business insights and performance metrics on demand

This walkthrough implements the Informational pattern (SharePoint document grounding) and the Transactional pattern (automated inbound delivery creation and warehouse task management) — showing how both can work together in a single Joule Capability Package built with Joule Studio in SAP Build.

This Demo — Solution Architecture

The diagram below shows how the components in this walkthrough connect at runtime. The Joule Capability Package (Custom Agent Ideation) sits on top of three reusable building-block packages and exposes four skills that Joule routes to based on the user's intent.

Imported Package Version Purpose

SharePoint Document Grounding Search1.1.0AI Core vector search over SharePoint
Delivery Note Assistant1.1.0SBPA action to retrieve and extract delivery note details
Inbound Delivery (A2X)1.1.3SBPA action to create inbound deliveries in S/4HANA

These three packages are SAP-maintained building blocks. You write only the orchestration logic that is unique to your scenario.

Custom Agent Ideation project overview showing imported package dependencies

Part 1: SharePoint Integration

Step 1 — Set Up SharePoint Grounding

This step produces the four credentials that AI Core needs to pull documents from SharePoint. You will set up a SharePoint site, register an Azure app, grant the right permissions, and validate access before wiring anything into AI Core.

1a — Prepare the SharePoint Site

  1. Sign in to sharepoint.com and create a new Communication site (recommended for document repositories).
  2. Upload your delivery note files. Supported formats include PDF, DOCX, PPTX, XLSX, and images.
  3. Note the site URL: https://<tenant>.sharepoint.com/sites/<site-name> — you will need it in Step 2.

1b — Register an Azure App in Microsoft Entra ID

  1. Go to Azure Portal → Entra ID → App registrations → New registration.
  2. Name the app (e.g., SAP-DocumentGrounding) and complete the registration.
  3. From the Overview page, copy the Application (client) ID and Directory (tenant) ID.
  4. Go to Certificates & Secrets → Client Secrets → New client secret.
    • Copy the Value immediately — it is shown only once. The Secret ID (GUID) is not the secret value.
  5. Go to API Permissions → Add a permission → Microsoft Graph → Application permissions.
    • Add Sites.Selected (Application type — "Access selected site collections").
    • Do not use Sites.Read.All — Sites.Selected is the least-privilege option and the correct permission for Document Grounding.
    • Click Grant admin consent. The Sites.Selected permission must show a ✅ status before you continue.
Caution: Admin consent requires a Global Administrator or Privileged Role Administrator. If you do not have that role, you cannot approve this yourself.
  • SAP employees: raise a request with your SharePoint / Azure Entra ID admin team to grant consent on the app registration.
  • External partners / customers: work with your organisation's SharePoint administrator to click Grant admin consent on your behalf.
Without this step, all API calls will return 403 Forbidden even if the permissions appear in the list.

Azure App Registration overview page showing Application and Directory (tenant) IDsAzure API permissions showing Sites.Selected granted with admin consent

1c — Grant the App Access to the SharePoint Site

Admin consent in Entra ID grants API-level access, but you also need to grant site-level read permission using PnP PowerShell:

Install-Module PnP.PowerShell

Connect-PnPOnline -Url "https://<tenant>.sharepoint.com/sites/<site-name>" -Interactive

Grant-PnPAzureADAppSitePermission `
  -AppId "<CLIENT_ID>" `
  -DisplayName "SAP Document Grounding" `
  -Site "https://<tenant>.sharepoint.com/sites/<site-name>" `
  -Permissions Read

1d — Validate Access (Optional but Recommended)

Get an access token and confirm the Graph API can reach your site before wiring it into AI Core:

# Get access token
curl -X POST "https://login.microsoftonline.com/<TENANT_ID>/oauth2/v2.0/token" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" \
  -d "grant_type=client_credentials&client_id=<CLIENT_ID>&client_secret=<CLIENT_SECRET>&scope=https://graph.microsoft.com/.default"

# Validate site access
curl -X GET "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/sites/<SHAREPOINT_DOMAIN>:/sites/<SITE_NAME>" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer <ACCESS_TOKEN>"

A 200 response with site metadata confirms everything is wired correctly.

At the end of this step you should have four values ready:

Value Where to find it

Tenant IDEntra ID → App registration → Overview
Client IDEntra ID → App registration → Overview
Client SecretEntra ID → Certificates & Secrets → Value column
SharePoint Site URLSharePoint Admin Center or browser address bar

These four values feed directly into the AI Core secret in the next step.

Part 2: AI Core Service Integration

Step 2 — Create the SharePoint Grounding Generic Secret in AI Launchpad

AI Core needs your SharePoint credentials to pull documents during indexing. Store them as a Generic Secret in SAP AI Launchpad so they are never hardcoded in pipeline configuration.

  1. Open SAP AI Launchpad → AI Core Administration → Generic Secrets.
  2. Click Create and name the secret, e.g., sharepoint-grounding-secret.
  3. Paste in the credentials JSON:
{
  "tenantId": "<AZURE_TENANT_ID>",
  "clientId": "<CLIENT_ID>",
  "clientSecret": "<CLIENT_SECRET>",
  "sharepointSiteUrl": "https://<tenant>.sharepoint.com/sites/<site>"
}

AI Launchpad Generic Secret form showing Application (Client) ID fieldAI Launchpad Generic Secret form showing Client Secret field

  1. Save. This secret will be referenced by the chunking pipeline in Step 3.

Step 3 — Create the Document Chunking Pipeline

The chunking pipeline reads documents from SharePoint, splits them into semantically meaningful chunks, embeds them, and writes them to a vector collection in AI Core.

  1. In AI Launchpad, navigate to Pipelines → Create Pipeline.
  2. Select the Document Grounding template.
  3. Set the source to SharePoint and reference the secret from Step 2.
  4. Set chunk size and overlap. Recommended starting values: 512 tokens, 50-token overlap.
  5. Name the target collection — e.g., delivery-notes.
  6. Run the pipeline and wait for completion. Note the final document and chunk counts.
Tip: If you update documents in SharePoint regularly, schedule the pipeline to run on a recurring cadence so the vector index stays current.

Step 4 — Verify the AI Core Search Endpoint

Once the collection is populated, verify the search endpoint is working. This is what the searchDeliveryNotes skill will call at runtime.

  1. In AI Launchpad, go to Grounding → Collections and select delivery-notes.
  2. Note the Collection ID — you will use this as the id in the grounding filter in Part 2, Step 5.
  3. Set the retrieval config: Top K = 5, Similarity threshold = 0.7.
  4. Run a test query — e.g., "delivery note vendor material PO number" — and confirm chunks return correctly.

The SharePoint Document Grounding Search package imported in the project wraps this endpoint as a callable Joule function.

 

Part 3: SAP Build

Step 5 — Build the Actions

Actions in SAP Build Process Automation (SBPA) wrap external API calls as reusable steps. The project imports two action packages.

5a — SharePoint Delivery Note Search (from Delivery Note Assistant package)

This action calls the AI Core Orchestration endpoint and returns the delivery note content as structured JSON. Under the hood it combines document grounding with an LLM extraction step in a single request.

  • Action identifier: post_getDeliveryNoteDetails
  • Destination: mocked_JOULEDELIVERYNOTEDEST (points to your AI Core endpoint)
  • Input: query (string) — the search phrase passed by the agent
  • Output: deliverycontentdetails — the extracted delivery note fields as a JSON string

The SBPA function is called asynchronously via the Joule skill integration gateway and uses long-polling (14 s timeout) to wait for the AI Core response.

The full request body sent to AI Core:

{
  "orchestration_config": {
    "module_configurations": {
      "llm_module_config": {
        "model_name": "gpt-4o",
        "model_params": { "max_tokens": 1000, "temperature": 0 }
      },
      "grounding_module_config": {
        "type": "document_grounding_service",
        "config": {
          "input_params": ["query"],
          "output_param": "groundingOutput",
          "filters": [
            { "id": "<YOUR_COLLECTION_ID>", "data_repository_type": "vector" }
          ]
        }
      },
      "templating_module_config": {
        "template": [
          {
            "role": "system",
            "content": "You are a delivery note extraction assistant. Extract all fields from the document context and return ONLY a valid JSON object with no markdown, no extra text, no code fences."
          },
          {
            "role": "user",
            "content": "Document context:\n{{?groundingOutput}}\n\nExtract all delivery note details and return as JSON."
          }
        ]
      }
    }
  },
  "input_params": { "query": "<natural language search query>" }
}

Key points to note:

  • filters.id — replace <YOUR_COLLECTION_ID> with the Collection ID from Step 4.
  • data_repository_type: "vector" — must be snake_case. Using dataRepositoryType (camelCase) returns a 400 error.
  • {{?groundingOutput}} — the AI Core Orchestration service injects retrieved document chunks here before calling the LLM.
  • The system prompt forces a clean JSON response with no markdown fences, because the downstream agent parses the content string directly.

5b — Create Inbound Delivery (from Inbound Delivery (A2X) package)

This action calls the S/4HANA A2X Inbound Delivery API to create the delivery record.

  • Action identifier: post_A_InbDeliveryHeader
  • Destination: mocked_inbound_delivery (points to your S/4HANA or API Hub endpoint)

Field Source in agent S/4HANA field

supplierdeliverydocumentDeliveryNoteNoDeliveryDocumentBySupplier
deliverydateDeliveryDate (ISO format)DeliveryDate
actualdeliveryquantityDeliveryQtyActualDeliveryQuantity
purchaseorderPONumberReferenceSDDocument
purchaseorderitemLineNoReferenceSDDocumentItem
batchnumberBatchNoBatch

Step 6 — Create the AI Agent Project in SAP Build

The project in SAP Build groups everything together. Create a new AI Agent Project and import the three dependency packages.

  1. In SAP Build, go to Lobby → Create → Build an Automated Process → AI Agent.
  2. Name it Custom Agent Ideation, set description to Customized Joule Agent for Public Cloud ERP.
  3. Import the three dependency packages:
    • SharePoint Document Grounding Search
    • Delivery Note Assistant
    • Inbound Delivery (A2X)
  4. Configure the system aliases under Settings:
    • SbpaService → destination: SAP_SBPA_API
    • APIAGENTCALLBACK → destination: AC_callback_api

These aliases let the function configuration in SAP Build refer to the SBPA integration gateway without hardcoding URLs.

Step 7 — Build the Delivery Note to Inbound Delivery Agent

This is the orchestration brain of the solution. The agent runs the full Search → Extract → Map → Create → Confirm flow automatically, without any user intervention.

Agent configuration:

agent: Delivery Note to Inbound Delivery Agent
base_model: gpt-5-minimal
iterations: 25
default_output_format: markdown

The automated process the agent follows:

  1. On any delivery note query, immediately call Search Delivery Notes with the query: "delivery note number, vendor, material, quantity, PO number, line item"
  2. Parse the JSON response and extract: DeliveryNoteNo, DeliveryDate, PONumber, LineNo, MaterialNo, DeliveryQty, BatchNo, Warehouse
  3. Map the extracted values to the inbound delivery API fields (see table in Step 5b)
  4. Call Create Inbound Delivery with the mapped parameters
  5. Reply to the user with a confirmation including the delivery note number, PO, quantity, and date

The agent instruction explicitly states: "The agent must never ask the user for any of these values or present options; the process must be strictly automated and sequential."

Content safety filtering is applied to all inputs (Azure Content Safety, threshold 4 for hate/self-harm/sexual/violence).

Expertise:

You are an expert in automating logistics workflows, specializing in the seamless extraction, mapping, and orchestration of delivery note data to inbound delivery processes without user intervention.

Instructions:

**Purpose & Objectives**
The Delivery Note to Inbound Delivery Agent assists users in fully automating the creation of inbound deliveries from delivery notes. Its objectives are:
- Automatically search for and extract delivery note information upon receiving relevant queries.
- Parse, map, and transfer all required delivery fields for inbound delivery creation, without requesting further input from the user.
- Ensure successful creation is promptly confirmed to the user.

**Process**
1. When the user requests a delivery note-related task (such as providing a delivery note number, vendor, material, PO, or line item):
   - Immediately call the 'Search Delivery Notes' tool with the query: "delivery note number, vendor, material, quantity, PO number, line item".
2. Upon receiving the JSON output from the tool:
   - Parse the JSON string and extract these fields:
     - DeliveryNoteNo
     - DeliveryDate
     - PONumber
     - LineNo
     - MaterialNo
     - DeliveryQty
     - BatchNo
     - Warehouse
   - Convert *DeliveryDate* with same format of '2026-04-27T00:00:00'
   - Map the extracted values as follows for the next step:
     - SupplierDeliveryDocument = DeliveryNoteNo
     - DeliveryDate = DeliveryDate
     - ActualDeliveryQuantity = DeliveryQty
     - PurchaseOrder = PONumber
     - PurchaseOrderItem = LineNo
     - BatchNumber = BatchNo
3. Using the mapped values, automatically invoke the 'Create Inbound Delivery' tool with the mapped input parameters.
4. Upon successful execution, confirm to the user what was created (including delivery note and PO details).
- The agent must never ask the user for any of these values or present options; the process must be strictly automated and sequential as above.

**Tooling**
- 'Search Delivery Notes': Retrieves delivery note and related document data based on query parameters.
- 'Create Inbound Delivery': Consumes extracted and mapped values to generate inbound deliveries.
- JSON Parsing: The agent must be capable of handling and extracting the required fields from complex JSON responses and mapping them precisely as outlined.
- The orchestration logic must guarantee a hands-free, predictable workflow for the user.

Additional Context:

**Communication**
- Maintain a professional, concise, and transactional tone; avoid any unnecessary small talk or ambiguity.
- Always state exactly what was done and which identifiers were involved.
- Never prompt the user for information or present choices about delivery note or inbound delivery details.

**Guidelines**
- If any required value is missing in the JSON output, clearly state which information was unavailable, but do not request user input to supply it.
- Follow the sequence of tools and logic strictly: Search → Extract → Map → Create → Confirm.
- Avoid speculation or assumptions—work strictly with the output you have.

**Examples**
**User:** "Please process this delivery note."
**Agent:** *[Searches delivery notes, extracts all required fields, creates inbound delivery, then replies:]*
"Inbound delivery created for Delivery Note #54321 (PO #98765, Qty: 100, Date: 2024-06-10)."

**User:** "Can you make an inbound delivery from this note?"
**Agent:** *[Performs end-to-end flow automatically; responds with confirmation of creation and details.]*

**Edge Case:**
**User:** "Process this delivery note."
**Agent:** *[If deliveryNoteNo is missing after parsing JSON:]*
"Unable to create inbound delivery: delivery note number was not found in the search results."

Step 8 — Create the Joule Skills (Scenarios)

Skills are what Joule sees — each scenario maps a user intent to a function, which in turn calls an agent or directly invokes an action. The project exposes four skills:

Skill (Scenario) Joule trigger description Target function

searchDeliveryNotesUser asks about delivery notes, vendors, materials, or shipmentssearchDeliveryNotes (direct action)
deliveryNotetoInboundDeliveryUser wants to convert a delivery note to an inbound deliverydeliveryNotetoInboundDelivery (agent-orchestrated)
createInboundDeliveryUser wants to create an inbound delivery directlycreateInboundDelivery (direct action)
warehouseTaskCreatorUser wants to create a warehouse taskwarehouseTaskCreator (agent-orchestrated via MCP)

Each agent-orchestrated skill works the same way at the function level: take the raw user message, pass it to the agent, and return the agent's response as markdown.

# deliveryNotetoInboundDelivery function (simplified)
- type: agent-request
  name: deliveryNotetoInboundDelivery
  body: <? agentRequestBody ?>
  result_variable: agentResponse

Step 9 — Build the Warehouse Task Creator Agent (MCP)

The Warehouse Task Creator is a second agent that handles a different part of the workflow: once an inbound delivery exists, create the corresponding warehouse task via the MCP server.

Agent configuration:

agent: Warehouse Task Creator
base_model: gpt-5-minimal

Process:

  1. Receive a user request containing an inbound delivery number
  2. Validate that the number is present and correctly formatted (numeric)
  3. Call the MCP warehouse task creation tool
  4. Return the task ID, or a clear error message if something goes wrong

MCP toolkit binding:

toolkits:
  - name: WarehouseTaskCreator
    type: mcp
    config:
      description: Create warehouse task based on inbound delivery document number.
      destination: warehouse-task-mcp
      namespace: warehouseTaskCreator.WarehouseTaskCreator0
      path: /mcp
      timeout: 60

The type: mcp is what distinguishes this agent from the previous one. Instead of calling an SBPA action group, it calls a Model Context Protocol endpoint at /mcp on the warehouse-task-mcp destination. Joule discovers available tools dynamically via the protocol — new warehouse operations can be added to the MCP server without touching the agent definition.

Expertise:

You are an expert in creating warehouse tasks in the MCP system, specializing in processing inbound delivery numbers and efficiently triggering warehouse operations based on user requests.

Instructions:

**Purpose & Objectives**
The Warehouse Task Creator assists users in automatically creating new warehouse tasks in the MCP system using the inbound delivery number provided in their prompt.
Success criteria include:
- Promptly creating a warehouse task whenever a valid inbound delivery number is given.
- Ensuring the user receives clear confirmation about task creation, including task details or error messages.

**Process**
1. Receive the user's request containing an inbound delivery number.
2. Validate that the inbound delivery number is present and correctly formatted.
3. When the number is valid, call the MCP warehouse task creation tool and submit the inbound delivery number as input.
4. Relay the response from the MCP tool back to the user, summarizing task details or clearly stating any encountered issues.
5. If input is missing or invalid, ask the user for clarification or a valid inbound delivery number.

**Tooling**
- MCP Warehouse Task Creation Tool: Use this tool to create a warehouse task by inputting the provided inbound delivery number. Always match the user's request to the required format before calling the tool.

Additional Context:

**Communication**
- Keep responses concise, straightforward, and informative.
- Avoid ambiguity—if the inbound delivery number is missing or incorrect, state the issue directly.

**Guidelines**
- Never guess or invent inbound delivery numbers; always ask the user to provide one if missing or unclear.
- Confirm task creation clearly and include key details or relevant error messages.

**Examples**
**User:** "Create a warehouse task for inbound delivery 5273849"
**Agent:** "A new warehouse task has been created for inbound delivery 5273849. Task ID: WT-00012345."
**User:** "Create a task for inbound delivery 123xyz"
**Agent:** "The inbound delivery number '123xyz' is invalid. Please provide a valid numeric inbound delivery number."

Step 10 — Configure the MCP Destination

The MCP server exposes warehouse operations as MCP-compliant tool definitions. The BTP destination is what connects the SAP Build agent to your deployed server.

  1. Deploy the MCP server to BTP (Cloud Foundry or Kyma). The tool namespace is warehouseTaskCreator.WarehouseTaskCreator0.
  2. In BTP Cockpit, create a destination named warehouse-task-mcp pointing to your deployed MCP service URL.
  3. Configure authentication (Bearer token recommended).
  4. In SAP Build, the warehouseTaskCreator agent picks up this destination automatically via the type: mcp toolkit binding.
  5. Test end-to-end by triggering the warehouseTaskCreator skill in Joule with a real inbound delivery number.

End-to-End Walkthrough

With everything deployed, here is what a warehouse operator experiences in Joule:

Scenario A — Search for a delivery note:

"Show me delivery note from Vendor ABC for PO 4500012345"

Joule routes to searchDeliveryNotes → SBPA calls AI Core Document Grounding Search → returns chunks from the SharePoint PDF → Joule displays the delivery note details.

Scenario B — Fully automated inbound delivery creation:

"Process delivery note 54321"

Joule routes to deliveryNotetoInboundDelivery → agent searches SharePoint → extracts and maps all fields automatically → calls S/4HANA A2X API → confirms:

"Inbound delivery created for Delivery Note #54321 (PO #98765, Qty: 100, Date: 2026-04-27)."

Scenario C — Warehouse task creation:

"Create a warehouse task for inbound delivery 5273849"

Joule routes to warehouseTaskCreator → agent validates the number → calls MCP server → confirms:

"A new warehouse task has been created for inbound delivery 5273849. Task ID: WT-00012345."

Summary

Step Component What Was Built

1Azure / SharePointApp Registration with Sites.Selected for least-privilege grounding access
2AI LaunchpadGeneric Secret storing SharePoint credentials
3AI Core PipelineDocument chunking and embedding pipeline → delivery-notes vector collection
4AI Core SearchVector search endpoint verified and ready
5SAP Build Actionspost_getDeliveryNoteDetails (SharePoint) + post_A_InbDeliveryHeader (S/4HANA A2X)
6SAP Build ProjectCustom Agent Ideation with three imported packages
7Joule AgentdeliveryNotetoInboundDelivery — automated Search → Extract → Map → Create
8Joule SkillsFour scenarios: search, end-to-end automation, direct create, warehouse task
9Warehouse AgentwarehouseTaskCreator — MCP-powered agent for warehouse operations
10MCPwarehouse-task-mcp destination + deployed MCP server

Key Design Decisions

Why two separate agents instead of one?

The delivery note flow is deterministic and fully automated — it always follows Search → Create with no branching. The warehouse task flow is simpler and uses a completely different backend (MCP vs. SBPA). Keeping them separate means each agent has a tight, auditable task definition and an independent iteration budget.

Why MCP for warehouse tasks?

MCP lets the warehouse operations backend evolve independently. New tools — cycle counting, pick confirmation, stock queries — can be added to the MCP server without touching the Joule agent definition. Joule discovers new tools dynamically via the protocol.

Why import packages instead of building from scratch?

The three imported packages (SharePoint Document Grounding Search, Delivery Note Assistant, Inbound Delivery (A2X)) are SAP-maintained building blocks. Using them gives you tested action definitions and upgrade paths for free. You write only the orchestration logic that is unique to your scenario.

Next Steps

  • Extend the document scope — add more SharePoint libraries (purchase orders, goods receipts) by adding source configurations to the chunking pipeline.
  • Chain the scenarios end-to-end — route deliveryNotetoInboundDelivery output directly into warehouseTaskCreator so that a single Joule prompt covers the entire inbound process.
  • Add locale formatting — the project already includes a localize function that formats dates and numbers according to the user's regional settings before displaying results.

Related Links

  • SAP Architecture Center — Integrating and Extending Joule
  • SAP AI Core Documentation — Document Grounding
  • SAP Build Process Automation — Joule Studio
  • SAP Joule Extensibility Overview
  • Model Context Protocol (MCP) — Official Specification
  • Microsoft Graph API — Sites.Selected Permission
  • PnP PowerShell — Grant-PnPAzureADAppSitePermission

Have questions or feedback? Drop a comment below — happy to help. You can also find me and my teammates (liling.jia@sap.com, xiaoming.chai@sap.com) on the SAP Community profile page.

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Test Automation Tool in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Edition CE2608
entry.content:
Introduction

Every SAP S/4HANA Cloud release brings exciting innovations and continuous business improvements. While these enhancements deliver greater value to customers, they also introduce the need to validate the business processes continue to function seamlessly after every SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud release cycles (6-month release upgrade or monthly update).

Testing therefore becomes an essential part of every upgrade/update cycle. However, executing the complete regression suite for every upgrade/update is both time-consuming and resource-intensive. The key challenge is no longer whether to test but what to test and with what data and infer the tests with the right status.

As we continue to innovate and enhance the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Test Automation Tool (TAT), the CE2608 release brings in further improvements aimed at simplifying test scoping, test data management, test status comparison of different Post Upgrade Tests (PUT) executions

In this blog, I’ll walk you through the following latest features delivered with CE2608.

  • AI-Powered Test Scoping in Intelligent Test Scoper (i-TS)
  • Multiple Data Variant capabilities for Test Data
  • Post Upgrade Tests (PUT) Comparator enhancements

1)    AI-Powered Test Scoping in Intelligent Test Scoper (i-TS)

To address the challenge of deriving the right scope automatically, Intelligent Test Scoper (i-TS) was introduced to automatically derive an optimized PUT scope based on SAP Standard code changes delivered in every major upgrade or monthly update.

While code-change analysis marked a major step towards intelligent regression testing, in this release we have introduced an additional optimization measure with AI-based scoping complementing the code-change-based approach.

Several additional factors like below are now considered whether a business process should be executed after an upgrade where AI has the potential to identify patterns that traditional rule-based approaches cannot

  • Customer Relevancy: Application usage information indicating how frequently customers execute individual business processes.
  • Process Complexity: The number of ABAP development objects touched by each automated test process across different object types
  • Code Change Object Ratio: The relationship between changed development objects and the total objects touched by an automation process
  • Historical Behaviour: Regression behaviour observed across previous releases

What's New in CE 2608

Based on the above factors, the AI model was trained and validated with simulated upgrade scenarios of previous releases to identify the right scope. Beginning with the SAP S/4HANA Cloud CE2608 release, Intelligent Test Scoper proposes an optimized regression scope using AI taking the below factors into consideration.

  • SAP Standard code changes
  • Customer Usage
  • Process complexity
  • Change-to-touch ratio
  • Historical learning from previous cloud releases

The result is a smarter regression scope that enables customers to:

  • Execute fewer but relevant regression tests without compromising on quality
  • Reduce upgrade validation effort
  • Shorten regression execution time
